title: ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO
exclusions: SIEM planned maintenance windows; new data source baselining periods; automated triage closures; analyst capacity gaps during documented training periods; detection rule tuning windows.
evidence: Monitoring coverage report showing data sources, detection rules, alert volumes, investigation outcomes, and effectiveness metrics for ISMS management review.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.30 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.30 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.15: ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.15' running on netbackup:job, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Disable a test log forwarder on a security-relevant host; verify the monitoring search detects the forwarding gap within 30 minutes and generates an alert with hostname, expected data, and last-received timestamp.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Confirm all test log forwarders are active and sending data within expected intervals; verify no forwarding gap alert fires and the monitoring dashboard shows all sources as healthy.

spl:
index=backup sourcetype=netbackup:job earliest=-14d
| lookup app_rpo_hours.csv policy_name OUTPUT rpo_hours
| eval end_epoch=_time+duration_sec
| eval overrun=if((end_epoch-_time)/3600>rpo_hours,1,0)
| where status!="success" OR overrun=1
| stats count by policy_name, client_name
description: Compares backup job duration and completion time against RPO windows for ICT supporting business continuity.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes backup and sourcetypes netbackup:job. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize duration fields; (2) tune for incremental chains; (3) feed BC tests. Indexes required: `index=backup`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=netbackup:job`; Lookups: `app_rpo_hours`, `app_rpo_hours.csv`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **system maintenance and upgrades** — Monitoring gaps during planned SIEM upgrades, parser updates, or search head restarts documented in the maintenance schedule.

2. **Data source onboarding baseline** — New data sources generating unfamiliar patterns during the initial baselining period before detection rules are tuned.

3. **Alert rule tuning periods** — Elevated false positive rates during detection rule refinement cycles that are part of the continuous improvement process.

4. **Analyst capacity constraints** — Queue depth increases during known capacity gaps (training, conferences) with documented risk acceptance for the period.

5. **Automated triage resolution** — Events resolved by SOAR playbooks without human queue entry are properly handled but not visible in manual review metrics.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `backup` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `app_rpo_hours.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('app_rpo_hours.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.5.30`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.30 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.15: ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `backup` under `netbackup:job`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Normalize duration fields. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 2:** tune for incremental chains. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** feed BC tests. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the ISO 27001 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `app_rpo_hours.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`rpo_hours`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `policy_name`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `app_rpo_hours.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval policy_name = lower(policy_name)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 14 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.15: ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.15,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=netbackup:job earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `client_name`, `end_epoch`, `overrun`, `policy_name`. Run:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=netbackup:job earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table client_name, end_epoch, overrun, policy_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `app_rpo_hours.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup app_rpo_hours.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.15: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for ICT Readiness for BC — Backup Window Overruns vs RPO. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
